Hey guys, welcome to module 6. So at this point in the training you've launched your product and you've generated momentum right out of the gates with a powerful cart open email. And maybe you even got ambitious and added a fast action cart open bonus like we also spoke about in module 5. And even though this makes for a really great start, it's by no means time to take your foot off the accelerator. Because the truth is most people won't buy the moment you open up your cart. It remains true that no matter how well you executed the first part of your launch, most of your sales are still going to come during the sales and closing sequences. Now before we go deeper, you might be wondering why I just separated those two. What is the difference between a sales and closing sequence? I mean most marketers and copywriters just kind of lump that in in the same general category as follow up emails. But in this course, especially when we're trying to coach the conversion, there is a distinction to be made and a pretty important one at that. So on the surface here is the difference. The sales sequence will start right after cart open and conclude 48 hours before the close at which point your closing sequence will clock in for duty and do its job to maximize conversions right up until the final buzzer. And this distinction is necessary because while they play similar roles, there are some crucial differences. It's just kind of like how some types of emails will perform much better during the close than they would the sales sequence and vice versa. And part of what we're doing in coaching the conversion is identifying and really tapping into what kind of messages our audience will resonate with and which ones they need to hear at what time, in what order, in order to move closer to and eventually beyond that buyer's line. One of the best ways probably to kind of illustrate the difference between the two is how, whereas in a closing sequence, you'll definitely want to be leveraging and using time urgency leading with it when the clock still has more than two days remaining is actually counterproductive. And that's a major mistake that I see a lot of marketers make is you'll get those emails or messages that say four days left until we take it away 72 hours left. And all that's really doing is it's not creating urgency. It's coaching complacency and complacency is obviously not what we're going for here. Now the other big mistake that comes up a lot in the sale sequence is marketers will make the mistake of diluting their sales messages by adding new ones. You know, a lot of the times we feel that if someone didn't buy it right out of the gates, it means they weren't responding to our initial set of beliefs, reframes and sales arguments, which is not the case. Or at least you can't fully know that until your launch is complete and you do a full post mortem. You really do need to allow your sale sequence and your closing sequence to do their jobs and to reinforce those sales arguments. And of course, the closing sequence to coach that decisiveness before making any kind of assessment of whether or not your sales arguments and the way you presented them and beliefs and attitudes and shifts and all that were appropriate or not. So that's another big mistake. The sale sequence isn't the time to throw more beliefs, outcomes and sales arguments against the wall to see what sticks. It's time to double down on the ones you've already made. And we do this by really reinforcing those arguments with proof, with case studies, just backing them up in a stronger way, building an irrefutable case. And another way we do this in a very necessary way is those beliefs are now recontextualized with your offer, with your product as the mechanism that catalyzes it, that really leverages those beliefs you've instilled. Because you believe X, product Y is what now makes sense for you. So we are taking those beliefs they've already bought into and recontextualizing them with your product. Now it's important to say before we go deeper into this that no matter how well you execute your sale sequence, statistically speaking, it's still going to be the slowest part of your launch. But that doesn't mean it's not important and doesn't mean we could just kind of mail it in and send a few emails and call it a day. Because done well, what we're still doing here is we're chipping away. We're setting up the close. This is our opportunity to reinforce all the groundwork we've done in that pre-launch instead of diluting it, of course. And then from there we can lean on a powerful closing sequence that will really help your prospect down that final leg of that journey. So if the job of the closing sequence is to pull your prospect across, what is this marketing dead zone between launch and 48 hours remaining meant to accomplish? What are we actually coaching here? And there are four things. The first thing is identification with being a buyer. For one, we really need our prospect at this point in our launch to start seeing themselves as a buyer, to identify in a very real, grounded, and practical way the similarities between them and other people who have raised their hand and said, yes, I do want to complete this journey. So when we get into the theme plates here, you're going to see one called the upgraded FAQ, which is a really great way to coach that identification. Another one you're going to see is one that you could use for your case studies called the curious case. And that one is going to be a unique way of presenting a case study in a way that really draws in curiosity and also elicits that identification with a buyer. Either your prospect seeing a case study and saying, that's so me, or seeing that case study and saying, that's exactly who I want to be. I feel I can become that person through your product. But moving on, we're also coaching recommitment and the closing of the consistency loop. Because we reflected back intent in the confirmation series and reaffirmed commitment to those outcomes, this is where we get to cash in on that. This is where we get to close that consistency loop and to really remind them, remind our prospect of the outcomes they've committed to and gently hold them accountable to it. So this is where we're closing that consistency loop. We close it in the sales sequence and then we tighten it even more in the closing sequence. Another thing we're coaching is the risk revealer. Many of us have heard of something called risk reversal, which you'll see more of in the closing sequence. It's definitely something we still use, but it's lesser known corollary is what I call risk, risk revealer. And what this is, is it's just bringing your prospect closer, more intimate to the stakes of going backwards. It's reminding them that stepping back is going back into that burning house, that the old way has a greater cost, more friction than moving forward. So that is the risk we are revealing. And to really coach this and really ground it and instill it in your prospect, we're going to explore two theme plates on this one. We're going to have the point of no return and the blind eye. And then finally, last but not least, as we alluded to earlier in this lesson, this module, this sequence is really coaching reinforcement of beliefs, reframes and outcomes with your product as the conduit, as a thing or mechanism that delivers it. So it's not giving them a million other new things to think about. That's actually one of the things that will stop people from buying is when you confuse them, when you give them too many things to think about, that gets in the way of decisiveness. So we're not going to be doing that. Instead, we're going to be giving them a new context of which those beliefs now exist. That exists within the context of your product. So that's the final thing. Just don't dilute your sales messages and reframes and set reinforced and padded with proof from your case studies, personal anecdotes, and how your product syncs up with this new vision and is in line with all these beliefs and these outcomes and these realities that you've created together. So that's what this sequence is selling. Once again, it's not about putting on surface level urgency and warning them that there's 72 hours left because that is nothing but a reprieve. That is just coaching complacency. However, there is going to be a little conditional thing that I want you to consider adding here. You know, especially if you're doing an extended launch where your sale sequence could last three or four or even seven days, I highly recommend adding a mid-card bonus, which will add some legitimate urgency, as well as really help kind of overcome that dreaded lull that can happen between your initial card open and your card close. So for these mid-card bonuses, you know, I've worked with clients who've used a wide array and different types of them, really. And the ones that I recommend, the ones I've seen get the best results is, I mean, first of all, something that helps them immediately after the course or using our terminology, something that after completing our course will exclusively empower them to use that next thing. So for example, if you were selling a course on creating courses, your mid-card bonus might be a small training on how to sell it. That would be the immediate next step. If you were teaching a beginner guitar player how to play the essential chords, your bonus might include a training on how to start combining them, how to transition between those chords so that you can play songs. And what this does is on a subtle yet powerful level, it presupposes a successful outcome. It gets your prospect thinking of what's beyond and the availability of new and exciting options and outcomes that exist for them after they achieve what your course or program has promised. And that's very powerful. It presupposes success. It presupposes that they're going to be in that training and accomplish the outcome that you've been promising them. Now a second option is actually going to tie in really, really well with your case study and you're going to see how to do that in the curious case theme plate. And the point here is to really show an exciting process or result that you spoke about or that a testimonial or previous client outlined in the case study and then introduce a bonus that supports that further. So it supports your core material in generating that specific outcome from the case study. So for example, let's say your case study was about a guitar player who after just three weeks was able to play Green Day's Time of Your Life around the campfire, which made him feel awesome. You can include a special bonus that breaks down the exact technique of how to play that song really fast, how to learn how to play that song really fast and speed that up. So those are the two mid-card bonuses that I see work really well. I would recommend starting with the first one, something that would really help them after they get the main promise, after they've completed the main part of your course because that presupposes the successful outcome. And the second one, if you are using a case study and if that case study has a certain outcome that could be further supported, you know, beyond what's in your core training, then I would really recommend including a bonus that just complements your core training in getting them that result. So with all that said, I know there is a lot in this module, but we are going to take a break and then we are going to explore these theme plates in greater detail, which will show you how to activate these principles and really coach them in a practical way through your email messaging. So let's take a break and I will meet you in the workbook.
